request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","name":"Knewton","one_liner":"Adaptive learning SaaS platform personalizing educational content for schools, publishers, and higher-ed institutions, founded 2008 and acquired for distressed price in 2019.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both are B2B SaaS platforms licensed to institutional customers (schools/publishers vs. academic spinouts/CROs) on a recurring-subscription basis. The licensing-to-institutions model is structurally similar, but Knewton's eventual pivot to courseware blurred its pure-platform positioning in a way the new pitch does not plan to replicate."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Knewton served the K-12 and higher-education edtech market; the new pitch targets biotech/life-sciences (wet-lab assay management). These are entirely different verticals with different regulatory environments, buyer personas, and competitive landscapes. Similarity is limited to both being knowledge-work B2B markets."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Knewton relied heavily on partnerships with large publishers (Pearson, Houghton Mifflin) to reach end institutions, which created dependency risk. The new pitch targets academic spinouts and small CROs directly — a more bottoms-up, niche-first GTM — though both rely on enterprise-style licensing."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Knewton raised over $150M across multiple VC rounds and was at significant scale before failing. The new pitch appears to be early-stage. The structural pattern (raise institutional VC, license platform to institutions) is similar in concept but the new pitch is at far earlier stage."}},"why_similar":"Both are B2B SaaS platforms sold to institutional buyers on a recurring-subscription model. Both operate as enabling infrastructure (Knewton for content personalization; the new pitch for wet-lab assay management) that customers embed into their workflows rather than buy as a point solution. Both target relatively niche institutional segments (higher-ed vs. academic spinouts/small CROs) rather than mass-market enterprise. The core risk of platform commoditization and buyer-budget constraints is shared.","where_diverged":"1. Vertical: Knewton was edtech; the new pitch is biotech/life-sciences — entirely different regulatory, procurement, and competitive environments. 2. Customer size: Knewton ultimately chased large publishers and university systems; the new pitch explicitly targets small CROs and academic spinouts, implying a lower ACV and higher volume model. 3. Partnership dependency: Knewton bet existential go-to-market on a handful of major publishers; the new pitch's direct-to-small-customer GTM avoids that single-point-of-failure. 4. Product type: Knewton was a data/algorithm platform requiring content partners to deliver value; the new pitch is a self-contained operational tool (assay management) that delivers standalone utility without a content ecosystem.","failure_causes":["Over-reliance on a small number of large publisher partnerships for distribution","Inability to demonstrate clear, measurable ROI to institutional buyers at scale","Market commoditization as large EdTech incumbents (Pearson, McGraw-Hill) built or acquired competing adaptive-learning capabilities","Excessive capital raised (~$157M) relative to actual revenue traction, creating unsustainable valuation expectations","Pivot away from core platform-API model into courseware development diluted product focus","Long enterprise sales cycles in education exhausted runway before scale was achieved","Distressed acquisition for under $17M despite $157M raised, indicating terminal unit-economics failure"],"lessons_for_input":["Avoid betting distribution on a single large partner — build direct sales motion to your niche segment from day one so you are not held hostage by one buyer's roadmap.","Prove clear, quantifiable ROI (e.g., assay error rates, time-to-result, compliance hours saved) before scaling, because institutional buyers will not renew on promises alone.","Resist scope creep from platform tool into full-service offering — stay in your lane as infrastructure and let customers own their own workflows.","Right-size your fundraise to your actual revenue base; raising far more capital than your market can support inflates valuation and narrows exit options to near zero.","Target customers (academic spinouts, small CROs) who feel acute pain and have budget authority — validate willingness to pay before building deep feature sets."]}
